title: Services
narrativeIntro: Not everything belongs in a component. Some knowledge needs to be shared — data that multiple components access, logic that shouldn't be duplicated. The builders of The Drift centralized these in services: singleton providers that any component can request. The Drift gives them to you through injection. Ask, and you receive.
narrativeOutro: The architecture is maturing. Components for the view, services for the shared logic. Clean separation. The Drift's third cluster is taking shape — not just nodes, but connections, data flowing through defined channels.
phmConnectionNote: Ryland creates shared systems that multiple parts of the ship can access. You learn Angular services and dependency injection.
conceptsPreview: Angular services, @Injectable, dependency injection, and singleton scope

challenges.title: One Instance
challenges.narrativeHook: Two components. One service. How many instances of the service exist?
challenges.instructions:
A service is decorated with `@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })` and injected by two different components.

Without running it, predict how many instances of the service Angular creates.
challenges.solution:
// No code changes — this is a prediction challenge.
// Answer: 1 instance. log.logs has 2 items: ['A loaded', 'B loaded'].
challenges.predictPrompt: How many instances of LogService exist, and how many items are in logs?

explainGate.prompt: What does providedIn: 'root' mean, and why does it result in a single shared instance?

hints: providedIn: 'root' registers the service with the root injector — one instance for the entire application.
Both components receive the SAME instance of LogService from Angular's injector.
Both adds go to the same logs array → 2 items.

challenges.title: Build a Service
challenges.narrativeHook: The crew roster needs to be accessible from multiple places. Build the service that holds it.
challenges.instructions:
Complete the `CrewService`:

- `crew` is an array of strings, starting with `['Grace', 'Ilyukhina', 'Shapiro']`
- `add(name: string)` pushes the name into the array
- `remove(name: string)` removes the first matching entry
- `count` getter returns the current length

files.name: crew.service.ts

tests.testCode: const s = new CrewService(); if (s.count !== 3) throw new Error(`count should be 3, got ${s.count}`);
const s = new CrewService(); s.add('Chen'); if (s.count !== 4) throw new Error(`After add, count should be 4, got ${s.count}`);

tests.description: add() should append to crew array
tests.testCode: const s = new CrewService(); s.add('Chen'); if (!s.crew.includes('Chen')) throw new Error('crew should contain Chen after add');

const s = new CrewService(); s.remove('Grace'); if (s.count !== 2) throw new Error(`After remove, count should be 2, got ${s.count}`); if (s.crew.includes('Grace')) throw new Error('Grace should be removed');

hints: remove() needs to find the index first: const i = this.crew.indexOf(name); then splice: this.crew.splice(i, 1);

challenges.title: Inject Correctly
challenges.narrativeHook: The component tries to use the service, but the service is never injected. The property is undefined.
challenges.instructions:
This component uses a `SensorService` but `this.sensor` is always `undefined` at runtime.

There is exactly one bug: the service is not injected through Angular's DI system — it's manually constructed. Fix the injection.

files.name: sensor-display.component.ts

tests.testCode: const s = new SensorService(); if (Math.round(s.average * 10) / 10 !== 56.6) throw new Error(`average should be 56.6, got ${s.average}`);
const s = new SensorService(); if (s.readings.length !== 5) throw new Error(`readings should have 5 items, got ${s.readings.length}`);

tests.description: SensorDisplayComponent should use the injected service, not construct a new one
tests.testCode: const injected = new SensorService(); const c = new SensorDisplayComponent(injected); if (c.sensor !== injected) throw new Error('SensorDisplayComponent should receive SensorService via constructor injection — c.sensor should be the injected instance, not undefined or a new instance');

explainGate.prompt: What is the problem with using `new SensorService()` instead of constructor injection, and what does Angular's DI system provide that manual construction doesn't?

hints: Using new SensorService() creates a NEW instance, bypassing Angular's DI system. Any changes made to that instance won't be reflected in the shared root instance.
Angular injects services via the constructor: constructor(private sensor: SensorService) {}
Remove the ngOnInit and the manual assignment. Use constructor injection instead.

challenges.title: Thin Component
challenges.narrativeHook: The component is doing too much — fetching, filtering, formatting. Extract the logic into a service.
challenges.instructions:
The `PowerMonitorComponent` currently does all its logic inline. Move the business logic into `PowerService`:

- `PowerService` should hold `readings` array and expose:
  - `getReadings()` returning the array
  - `getMax()` returning the highest value
  - `getAverage()` returning the mean (rounded to 1 decimal)
- `PowerMonitorComponent` should inject `PowerService` and delegate to it

Behavior must stay identical.

files.name: power-monitor.component.ts

tests.testCode: const s = new PowerService(); if (s.getMax() !== 91) throw new Error(`getMax() should be 91, got ${s.getMax()}`);
const s = new PowerService(); if (s.getAverage() !== 68.2) throw new Error(`getAverage() should be 68.2, got ${s.getAverage()}`);
const s = new PowerService(); if (s.getReadings().length !== 6) throw new Error(`getReadings() should return 6 items, got ${s.getReadings().length}`);

hints: Move the readings array and the computation logic (Math.max, reduce) into PowerService as methods.
The component constructor should inject PowerService: constructor(private powerService: PowerService) {}
The component's max and average getters should delegate: return this.powerService.getMax(); and return this.powerService.getAverage();
